data.title: Weekly ETF flows: two of 11 sectors record outflows; Gold leads inflows
data.description:
The world's largest exchange-traded fund, SPDR S&P 500 Trust (SPY), saw inflows of $3.5B for the week ending April 17, while its price increased by 3.5%.

The SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD) recorded inflows totaling $1.93B last week as GLD prices increased 2.43% during the week. iShares Silver Trust ETF (SLV) also recorded inflows totaling $20.93M, while its price jumped over 7.8% during the week. The iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT) also registered inflows of $906.09M last week, while the Bitcoin (BTC-USD) price increased 2.10% over the same period.

Last week’s inflows/outflows

The 11 S&P 500 sector tracking ETFs collectively recorded inflows of about $3.11B last week, according to data from etfdb.com.

The highest inflows last week were seen in the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLE), totaling $1.14B, followed by the Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLK) with inflows of $875.53M. The Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLF) recorded an inflow of $706.48M last week.

Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLU) led sector outflows, as two out of 11 sectors saw money flowing out of their respective sector-wise funds. The Utilities Sector (XLU) saw an outflow of $539.04M, followed by Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY) with $134.52M flowing out last week.

data.title: T-Mobile US gains on report Deutsche Telekom evaluating combination
data.description:
T-Mobile US (TMUS) rose 1.5% after a report that Deutsche Telekom AG (DTEGY) is evaluating a full combination with its American arm.

The German carrier has been discussing creating a new holding company that would make a stock bid for shares of both Deutsche Telekom and T-Mobile (TMUS), according to a Bloomberg report on Tuesday, which cited people familiar with the matter. Deutsche Telekom already has a 53% stake in T-Mobile.

The combined company, which would be jointly owned by the companies' current investors, may look to list in the US and a major European exchange, according to the report. 

Deliberations are said to be at a preliminary stage and any deal would require political support to move forward, according to the report. 

“As per their usual practice, DT and TMUS do not comment on speculation regarding their corporate activity, nor are there specifics for us to comment on for your inquiry,” a spokesperson for Deutsche Telekom said in an emailed statement to Bloomberg. T-Mobile declined to comment. 

T-Mobile (TMUS) is set to report Q1 results next Tuesday.

data.title: Genuine Parts is still on track for a 2027 business separation
data.description:
Genuine Parts Company (GPC) reiterated on Tuesday that it is still on track to separate into two independent, publicly traded companies in 2027.

The planned transaction will carve the company into a dedicated global automotive business, centered on its NAPA-branded automotive parts distribution network, and a separate global industrial business built around its motion industrial parts and services operations. The split is intended to be tax‑free for U.S. shareholders and is expected to create two "industry‑leading" companies with sharper strategic focus, simplified operations, and the ability to pursue tailored capital allocation and investment agendas. 

The board of directors of Genuine Parts (GPC) does not need shareholder approval to complete the separation, which remains subject to customary conditions, including SEC review of a Form 10. Details on names, boards, and executive teams will come later.

Alongside its first-quarter earnings report, Genuine Parts (GPC) reaffirmed its full‑year 2026 guidance. Management described the upcoming separation as a continuation of a multi-year portfolio simplification and growth strategy designed to enhance long‑term shareholder value across both the automotive and industrial franchises.

Shares of Genuine Parts (GPC) are down more than 20% since tension in the Middle East disrupted the auto supplier sector.

data.title: Lithia Motors Q1 2026 dividend preview: Will April extend 15-year growth streak?
data.description:
Lithia Motors (LAD) is expected to announce a dividend increase this April, continuing its 15-year streak of continuous dividend growth.

Based on past patterns, analysts expect a consensus annual dividend of ~$2.26 per share, which brings the quarterly dividend to ~$0.565 per share. This clearly implies an increase of ~2.7% from the current ~$0.55 dividend payout.

The company last paid a dividend of ~$0.55 per share in January 2026, which comes to an annual yield of ~0.76%. However, earlier, in April last year, it raised the dividend by ~3.8%, from ~$0.53 to ~$0.55.

Over the past five years, the company has delivered a dividend growth rate of ~12.33% and currently maintains a payout ratio of ~6.44%.

Moreover, as per dividend metrics ratings, the company carries an A+ for safety, a C+ for growth, a D+ for yield, and a B+ for consistency.

Now, investors should keep a close watch on the Q1 2026 results, which will be released on Wednesday, April 29, 2026, before the market opens.

data.title: AI is emerging as a productivity engine, not a job killer - Morgan Stanley
data.description:
Artificial intelligence (AIQ) (AIEQ) is driving meaningful gains in U.S. labor productivity—but not through the job displacement many economists feared, according to a new analysis from Morgan Stanley Research released Tuesday.

According to analyst Katy Huberty, industries with high AI exposure contributed 1.7 percentage points of the 2.4 percentage point growth in output per employee across the U.S. in 2025. That marks a significant acceleration from just 0.7 percentage points in 2024.

The more surprising finding: these productivity gains are coming from faster output growth rather than workforce reductions.

“Trends in employment growth have been similar across industries with different degrees of AI exposure, while output growth has accelerated more rapidly in industries that we have identified as having higher AI exposure,” Huberty wrote in the note.

Tech-oriented sectors have led the charge, posting the strongest acceleration in both output and labor productivity. The dynamic suggests companies are using AI tools to produce more with their existing workforce rather than trimming headcount to achieve efficiency gains, offering early evidence that AI adoption may follow a more benign path than previous waves of automation, at least in the near term.

data.title: No significant changes expected with new Fed chair – Wells Fargo’s Paul Christopher
data.description:
Paul Christopher, head of global investment strategy at Wells Fargo, said he does not expect significant changes to monetary policy even amid potential leadership shifts at the Federal Reserve.

“We…wouldn’t expect markets to be riled,” Christopher said in an interview with CNBC.

The strategist emphasized that the institutional structure of the Federal Reserve serves as a safeguard against dramatic policy changes. Christopher explained that any major shift would require convincing nearly two dozen officials, noting there are “18 other people that need to be persuaded, plus the staff, and it just doesn’t seem like that’s a task that anyone really wants to try to take on.”

Turning to energy markets, Christopher predicted that the sharp rebound in WTI crude prices (CL1:COM) since late March will inevitably unwind.

“I’m not saying that WTI is going to $120 or $150,” he clarified, but added that “markets have been trading on momentum here, and the momentum has been following headlines.”

Christopher cautioned investors against reacting too strongly to day-to-day diplomatic developments and geopolitical news.

He described the current market environment as being on a “treadmill of following headlines too closely and extrapolating from them,” urging investors instead to focus on what matters most: “earnings and valuations.”

Wells Fargo’s current guidance recommends that investors consider trimming energy positions in favor of technology stocks (VGT), (XLK), (IYW). Christopher noted that the information technology sector “has underperformed energy (XLE), (AMLP), (VDE) over the past several weeks,” adding that the fundamentals do not fully support energy’s recent outperformance.

Rather than attempting to pick individual AI winners, Christopher advised taking broad exposure to the information technology sector.

He highlighted that tech valuations have become more attractive, pointing out that “you’re not paying anymore a premium for tech over the rest of the index.”

Tech ETFs: (VGT), (XLK), (IYW), (FTEC), (IXN), and (RSPT).

data.title: Invesco dividend preview: April raise seen extending 4-year run ahead of Q1 results
data.description:
Invesco (IVZ) is anticipated to announce a dividend increase this April, extending its 4-year streak of continuous dividend growth.

Based on historical charts and patterns, analysts expect a consensus annual dividend of $0.88 per share, implying a quarterly dividend of ~$0.22, which would represent a ~4.8% increase from the current $0.21 distribution.

The company last declared a dividend of $0.21 per share in January 2026, representing an annual yield of 3.37%, and raised its dividend last April by ~2.4% to $0.21 from $0.2050.

The investment management company has delivered a 5-year dividend growth rate of ~6.26% and maintains an average payout ratio of ~41.38%.

Moreover, as far as dividend metrics are concerned, the company holds ratings of C+ for safety, D+ for growth, B for yield, and B for dividend consistency.

Invesco (IVZ) is all set to report its Q1 2026 results on Tuesday, April 28, 2026, before the market opens.

data.title: ServiceNow inches up after it closes Armis acquisition
data.description:
ServiceNow (NOW) shares were up 1% during Tuesday trading after the company announced it had closed on its acquisition of Armis.

The acquisition is expected to more than triple ServiceNow's market opportunity for security and risk solutions. Armis provides cyber exposure management and security solutions. The platform observes, protects, and manages cyber risk across connected assets in real time.

The $7.75B all-cash acquisition was announced in December 2025. 

Last month, ServiceNow closed on its acquisition of Veza, which is an AI-native identity security platform. That acquisition totaled approximately $1B.

"Armis gives us real-time, contextual awareness into the cyber risk of every connected asset, including the devices and systems that conventional tools were never built to see," said Amit Zavery, president, chief operating officer, and chief product officer at ServiceNow. "Armis gives us real-time, contextual awareness into the cyber risk of every connected asset, including the devices and systems that conventional tools were never built to see. Combined with Veza's identity intelligence, that signal flows into ServiceNow's Context Engine and AI Control Tower, turning exposure into automated remediation with governance and a full audit trail built in at every step."

These acquisitions are helping ServiceNow establish an "AI Center for Cyber Defense," which is a global hub dedicated to building the next-generation AI security stack and pioneering the transition from reactive security to autonomous, agentic cyber defense. 

ServiceNow is slated to deliver its first quarter 2026 financial results post-market on Wednesday, April 22. A consensus estimate expects ServiceNow to deliver an adjusted earnings per share of $0.97 and GAAP EPS of $0.53. Revenue is expected to total $3.75B. In the same quarter last year, ServiceNow reported adjusted EPS of $0.81 on revenue of $3.09B.

data.title: Home Bancorp outlines $23.3M-$23.7M quarterly noninterest expense in 2026 while positioning for further NIM expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Home Bancorp (HBCP) Q1 2026

Management view

  • Chairman, President & CEO John Bordelon highlighted quarterly profitability and margin momentum: "Yesterday afternoon, we reported first quarter net income of $11.4 million or $1.46 per share -- sorry, $1.45 per share." He added, "Net interest margin expanded to 4.16%" and said the quarter’s margin improvement was driven by funding costs, noting it was "driven by a 22 basis point decline in our cost of funds."
  • Bordelon described a near-term growth constraint in lending, saying, "Loans declined by 1% in the first quarter as paydowns continued to outpace new production" and added, "We continue to see customers delay projects and transactions while they wait for additional clarity on interest rates." He said the pipeline has improved, but cautioned that "the timing and pace of future loan growth remain difficult to predict."
  • On funding and liquidity posture, Bordelon said, "Total deposits increased by $54 million" and added that "core deposits increased $118 million" while "noncore CD declines" offset part of that gain; he also noted, "our loan-to-deposit ratio declined to approximately 90%, positioning us well for future growth."
  • Bordelon emphasized Texas expansion and the new Northwest Houston branch: "The new Northwest Houston branch opened during the quarter and gives us full service presence in one of the fastest-growing areas in the market."
  • Senior EVP & CFO David Kirkley attributed higher net interest income to structural balance sheet improvements: "Net interest income totaled $34.5 million in the first quarter" and called it "the highest quarterly net interest income in Home Bank's history." He added, "the average cost of interest-bearing deposits declined 22 basis points to 2.29%, while our overall cost of deposits declined by 16 basis points to 1.68%."

Outlook

  • Kirkley reiterated expectations for fee income, stating, "We continue to expect quarterly noninterest income to be in the range of $3.8 million to $4 million."
  • On operating expenses, Kirkley guided, "For the remainder of 2026, we expect quarterly noninterest expenses to be between $23.3 million and $23.7 million," while noting expenses should "increase modestly beginning in the second quarter as annual raises take effect and technology investments ramp up."
  • On margin direction, Kirkley said, "we continue to readvance additional opportunity for NIM expansion" and pointed to reinvestment and loan cash-flow dynamics: "We continue to see a positive spread of approximately 40 basis points on new loan originations versus pay downs" and "New investment yields were north of 4% in Q1 versus expected roll-off yield of 2.43% over the next 12 months."
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s prepared remarks, the company again framed NIM as supported by repricing cash flows, but the current call’s Q&A focused more on the scenario of no additional rate cuts and whether deposit costs are near a floor.

Financial results

  • Kirkley said net interest income increased linked to funding costs and mix, stating, "Net interest income totaled $34.5 million in the first quarter, an increase of $434,000 from the fourth quarter."
  • He detailed balance sheet actions, saying deposit growth allowed the bank to reduce wholesale funding: "we were able to repay all of our more expensive FHLB advances" and characterized the remaining quarter-over-quarter change as "a minor improvement of $3 million quarter over quarter."
  • On credit metrics, Kirkley reported, "Nonperforming loans increased $1.6 million to $35.8 million or 1.31% of total loans" and, separately, "We recorded a provision expense of $922,000." Bordelon added on asset quality, "Nonperforming assets increased during the quarter by $3.8 million" but said, "we continue to believe losses of these credits will be immaterial given the collateral protection and guarantor support."
  • On fees and costs, Kirkley said, "Noninterest income decreased by $260,000 to $3.7 million" and added, "Noninterest expense declined by $106,000 to $22.9 million." He also reported capital progression: "Tangible book value per share increased to $46.04 this quarter."

Q&A

  • Stephen Scouten, Piper Sandler & Co., Research Division asked about margin sensitivity if there are no rate cuts and whether that changes expectations for expansion; Senior EVP & CFO Kirkley responded, "without any rate cuts, we're still seeing expansion" and added, "deposits are probably without any further rate cuts probably around their floor," while Chairman, President & CEO Bordelon said, "the deposit side probably will dictate the pace of the growth of that NIM."
  • Scouten, Piper Sandler & Co., Research Division asked for color on loan production and demand; Bordelon said this quarter was "very typical" seasonally and added, "I think we'll see higher demand potentially in second and third quarter" while flagging that "geopolitical issues" could influence activity.
  • Scouten, Piper Sandler & Co., Research Division asked whether the stock’s run-up enables more M&A or a partnership with a larger institution; Bordelon responded, "I think M&A will come a little more into focus" and added, "with our stock price trading closer to [ 140 ] tangible, we think we can do a deal this year."
  • Joseph Yanchunis, Raymond James & Associates, Inc., Research Division asked about deposit competition; Kirkley described selective pressure, saying the bank moved "from 3.65% is our top rate to 3.85%" in some markets and added competitors in Houston were in "the 4% to 4.25% range."
  • Yanchunis, Raymond James & Associates, Inc., Research Division challenged whether expense guidance changed from last quarter; Kirkley replied, "I feel like I didn't change that guidance at all" and added, "I'll have to follow up with you individually on that."
  • Yanchunis, Raymond James & Associates, Inc., Research Division asked about pipeline and utilization; Kirkley said, "The pipeline increased about $30 million" and clarified, "It's about $122 million," while Bordelon described runoff tied to "reduction in our appetite for nonowner-occupied" and cited competitive pricing pressure.
  • Feddie Strickland, Hovde Group, LLC, Research Division asked the average rate on new loan production; Kirkley answered, "About 7%." Strickland also asked about workout credit trajectory; Bordelon pointed to process delays, saying, "the time it's taking to run these special assets through the process" has been the main issue and added, "it's just been a very low process over the last couple of years."
  • Strickland, Hovde Group, LLC, Research Division asked about sustaining noninterest-bearing deposit growth; Bordelon linked it to banker mix and strategy shift: "our focus changed about 4 years ago away from that to more of a C&I customer" and said deposit inflows are coming from "total relationships."

Sentiment analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone centered on rate-path uncertainty (NIM with “no cuts”), competitive deposit pricing, and clarity on expense guidance; questions repeatedly pressed for quantification and durability (pipeline size, CD pricing, credit workout path).
  • Neutral-to-slightly positive management tone in prepared remarks emphasized margin and funding improvements, while Q&A responses introduced more conditional framing around deposits and rate uncertainty, including Bordelon’s "I'm not sure exactly where deposit rates are going to have to go" and Kirkley’s characterization that deposits are near "their floor."
  • Versus the prior quarter, the current call’s tone shifted toward scenario analysis (no-cuts environment) and competitive deposit pricing detail, while continuing the prior quarter’s emphasis on M&A optionality tied to stock valuation.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 2026 showed slightly lower EPS than Q4 2025, while NIM moved higher to 4.16% from 4.06% previously discussed, with management attributing Q1’s change primarily to funding-cost reductions.
  • The loan narrative shifted from Q4’s statement that loans grew and management expected "growth in 2026 to be in the mid-single digits" to Q1’s report that loans declined 1% as paydowns outpaced production and that growth timing is “difficult to predict.”
  • Deposit commentary remained consistent in emphasizing mix improvement and loan-to-deposit reduction, while Q1 added more color on competitive CD pricing dynamics in Houston.
  • M&A posture was reiterated; Q1 repeated the linkage to stock trading near "[ 140 ] tangible" and added timing language: "we think we can do a deal this year."

Risks and concerns

  • Bordelon cited uncertainty impacting loan demand: "continued market volatility and uncertainty around interest rates" and said customers are delaying activity while waiting for "additional clarity on interest rates."
  • Credit risk centered on problem asset resolution speed; Bordelon said, "the biggest issue" is the lengthy timeline to work special assets, and Kirkley noted higher provisioning was "primarily due to changes in individual declared reserves associated with these downgraded credits."
  • Deposit competition risk was explicitly tied to pricing outliers; Kirkley pointed to competitors offering "4% to 4.25%" CDs in Houston and described targeted pricing responses.

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a quarter of margin and funding-cost progress—highlighting NIM of 4.16%, record quarterly net interest income of $34.5 million, and a lower loan-to-deposit ratio of about 90%—while acknowledging that loan growth remains constrained by paydowns and rate-driven customer delays. Executives framed the forward outlook around additional NIM repricing opportunity, stable go-forward ranges for noninterest income ($3.8 million to $4 million) and noninterest expense ($23.3 million to $23.7 million), continued Texas franchise buildout, and the potential to pursue M&A in 2026 given the stock trading closer to “[ 140 ] tangible,” alongside ongoing focus on managing credit resolution timelines and competitive deposit pricing pressure in Houston.
